section.title: The Levitical Cities
section.sectionRationale: The tribe that received no land of its own now receives its portion: forty-eight cities with pasturelands, scattered among all the tribes, so that the priests and Levites, whose inheritance is the LORD, dwell throughout Israel to teach and lead in worship. The chapter closes with one of the great summaries of the Bible: the LORD gave Israel all the land, gave them rest, and not one of His good promises failed. It is one movement, the Levitical cities crowned by the declaration of God's perfect faithfulness.
section.thesis: God settles the landless Levites in cities throughout every tribe, turning Levi's scattering into a blessing that spreads worship and instruction across the nation, and the chapter ends by declaring that God kept every promise, gave the land, and granted rest, so that not one of all His good words failed.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the Levites request their cities, verses 1 to 3; the forty-eight cities allotted among the tribes, verses 4 to 42; and the great summary of God's faithfulness, verses 43 to 45.

verses.text: Now the family heads of the Levites approached Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the heads of the other tribes of Israel
at Shiloh in the land of Canaan and said to them, “The LORD commanded through Moses that we be given cities in which to live, together with pasturelands for our livestock.”
So by the command of the LORD, the Israelites gave the Levites these cities and their pasturelands out of their own inheritance:

units.label: The forty-eight cities allotted among the tribes

verses.text: The first lot came out for the Kohathite clans. The Levites who were descendants of Aaron the priest received thirteen cities by lot from the tribes of Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin.
The remaining descendants of Kohath received ten cities by lot from the tribes of Ephraim, Dan, and the half-tribe of Manasseh.
The descendants of Gershon received thirteen cities by lot from the tribes of Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and the half-tribe of Manasseh in Bashan.
And the descendants of Merari received twelve cities from the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun.
So the Israelites allotted to the Levites these cities, together with their pasturelands, as the LORD had commanded through Moses.
From the tribes of Judah and Simeon, they designated these cities by name
to the descendants of Aaron from the Kohathite clans of the Levites, because the first lot fell to them:
They gave them Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron), with its surrounding pasturelands, in the hill country of Judah. (Arba was the father of Anak.)
But they had given the fields and villages around the city to Caleb son of Jephunneh as his possession.
So to the descendants of Aaron the priest they gave these cities, together with their pasturelands: Hebron, a city of refuge for the manslayer, Libnah,
Jattir, Eshtemoa,
Holon, Debir,
Ain, Juttah, and Beth-shemesh, nine cities from these two tribes, together with their pasturelands.
And from the tribe of Benjamin they gave them Gibeon, Geba,
Anathoth, and Almon, four cities, together with their pasturelands.
In all, thirteen cities, together with their pasturelands, were given to the priests, the descendants of Aaron.
The remaining Kohathite clans of the Levites were allotted these cities: From the tribe of Ephraim
they were given Shechem in the hill country of Ephraim (a city of refuge for the manslayer), Gezer,
Kibzaim, and Beth-horon, four cities, together with their pasturelands.
From the tribe of Dan they were given Elteke, Gibbethon,
Aijalon, and Gath-rimmon, four cities, together with their pasturelands.
And from the half-tribe of Manasseh they were given Taanach and Gath-rimmon, two cities, together with their pasturelands.
In all, ten cities, together with their pasturelands, were given to the rest of the Kohathite clans.

This is what the Levite clans of the Gershonites were given: From the half-tribe of Manasseh they were given Golan in Bashan, a city of refuge for the manslayer, and Beeshterah, two cities, together with their pasturelands.
From the tribe of Issachar they were given Kishion, Daberath,
Jarmuth, and En-gannim, four cities, together with their pasturelands.
From the tribe of Asher they were given Mishal, Abdon,
Helkath, and Rehob, four cities, together with their pasturelands.
And from the tribe of Naphtali they were given Kedesh in Galilee (a city of refuge for the manslayer), Hammoth-dor, and Kartan, three cities, together with their pasturelands.
In all, thirteen cities, together with their pasturelands, were given to the Gershonite clans.
This is what the Merarite clan (the rest of the Levites) were given: From the tribe of Zebulun they were given Jokneam, Kartah,
Dimnah, and Nahalal, four cities, together with their pasturelands.
From the tribe of Reuben they were given Bezer, Jahaz,
Kedemoth, and Mephaath, four cities, together with their pasturelands.
And from the tribe of Gad they were given Ramoth in Gilead, a city of refuge for the manslayer, Mahanaim,
Heshbon, and Jazer, four cities in all, together with their pasturelands.
In all, twelve cities were allotted to the clans of Merari, the remaining Levite clans.
For the Levites, then, there were forty-eight cities in all, together with their pasturelands, within the territory of the Israelites.
Each of these cities had its own surrounding pasturelands; this was true for all the cities.

units.label: The great summary of God's faithfulness
units.note: not one of all the LORD's good promises had failed

verses.text: Thus the LORD gave Israel all the land He had sworn to give their fathers, and they took {{yarash|possession}} of it and settled in it.
And the LORD gave them {{nuach|rest}} on every side, just as He had sworn to their fathers. None of their enemies could stand against them, for the LORD delivered all their enemies into their hand.
Not one of all the LORD’s good {{dabar|promises}} to the house of Israel had failed; everything was fulfilled.

yarash.gloss: To take possession of, inherit, occupy. The summary declares that Israel yarash the land, took possession of it and settled in it. The great conquest verb of the book here reaches its resolution: what God gave by promise, Israel entered and possessed, so that the gift and the possession meet, and the land sworn to the fathers is truly theirs.

nuach.gloss: To rest, settle, be at peace. The LORD gave Israel nuach, rest on every side, the goal toward which the whole conquest moved. This God-given rest fulfills the promise to the fathers and foreshadows the deeper rest that remains for the people of God in Christ, the true and final rest that Joshua's rest could only picture, Hebrews 4:8-9.

dabar.gloss: A word, promise, matter, thing. The climactic verse declares that not one good dabar of all the LORD had spoken failed; every word came true. God's word is utterly reliable; what He promises He performs, so that the whole book of conquest and allotment stands as proof that the LORD keeps His word to the letter, the ground of all faith.

basic.context: The tribe of Levi, set apart for the service of God, received no territory of its own, because the LORD Himself is their inheritance. But they still needed places to live. So now the other tribes give the Levites forty-eight cities with surrounding pasturelands, spread throughout the whole land, and including the six cities of refuge. This scattering was purposeful: the priests and Levites, living among every tribe, would teach God's law and lead in worship across the nation. Then comes one of the most important summaries in the whole Bible: the LORD gave Israel all the land He had sworn, they took possession of it and settled, He gave them rest on every side, and not one of all His good promises failed. The question underneath is: can God be trusted to keep His word?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, a city list that climaxes in a great theological summary. The Levitical cities show how worship and instruction were woven into the life of the settled nation. The closing verses, 43 to 45, are the interpretive high point of the book, declaring God's perfect faithfulness. We must hold this alongside the honest notes of incomplete conquest: the land was truly given and rest truly granted, yet fuller possession remained, so the rest points beyond itself to a greater rest in Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: The summary declares that Israel yarash, took possession of, the land; the LORD gave them nuach, rest, on every side; and not one good dabar, word or promise, of all the LORD had spoken failed.
basic.typology: Levi scattered among the tribes to spread worship and instruction foreshadows the people of God, a kingdom of priests, spread through the world to declare His praises. The rest God gave points to the Sabbath rest in Christ. And God's perfect keeping of His word points to Christ, in whom all God's promises are Yes.
basic.christ: The rest given to Israel foreshadows the rest Christ gives, the Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God, Hebrews 4. And the declaration that not one of God's promises failed points to Christ, in whom all the promises of God find their Yes and Amen, the faithful and true One who fulfills every word.
basic.god: God is faithful, keeping every promise to the letter, so that not one good word failed. He is the giver of the land and of rest, fulfilling His oath to the fathers. And He is worthy to be worshiped throughout the land, providing for the Levites so that His praise and His law would fill the nation.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 49 foretold Levi being scattered in Israel, turned here to blessing. Deuteronomy 33 describes Levi teaching Israel God's law. 2 Corinthians 1 says all God's promises are Yes in Christ. And Hebrews 4 shows the rest as a shadow of the rest in Christ.
basic.oneStory: The landless Levites are settled throughout Israel to fill the nation with worship and instruction, and the whole conquest and allotment is crowned by the declaration that God gave the land, gave rest, and kept every promise. This faithfulness points to Christ, in whom all God's promises are Yes, and the rest given foreshadows the eternal rest secured in Him.

context.literary: Chapter 21 completes the allotment section by providing for the Levites, who had been noted throughout as receiving no territorial inheritance. The city list is organized by the three Levitical clans, Kohath, Gershon, and Merari, and totals forty-eight cities, including the six cities of refuge. The chapter, and the whole first half of the book's settlement account, climaxes in the summary of verses 43 to 45, a resounding statement of God's faithfulness that forms one of the theological peaks of Joshua and answers the promises with which the book opened.
context.historical: Levi's lack of a land inheritance flowed from their special calling to serve at the sanctuary and teach the law, with the LORD Himself as their portion, Numbers 18:20. Distributing them in forty-eight cities across all the tribes meant that priestly instruction and worship leadership were available everywhere, turning Jacob's ancient word that Levi would be scattered, Genesis 49:7, into a means of blessing. The cities came with pasturelands for the Levites' livestock. The summary's claim of rest on every side describes the settled security Israel enjoyed under Joshua, even though pockets of Canaanites remained.
context.canonical: The scattering of Levi to teach and lead worship anticipates the New Testament vision of all God's people as a royal priesthood spread through the world to proclaim His excellencies, 1 Peter 2:9. The summary that not one of God's good promises failed is echoed by Joshua's later farewell, 23:14, and undergirds the whole biblical confidence that God keeps His word, fulfilled supremely in Christ, in whom every promise is Yes, 2 Corinthians 1:20. The rest God gave becomes a key type in Hebrews for the greater Sabbath rest that remains in Christ, since the land-rest was real but not final.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the provision of forty-eight cities for the landless Levites throughout Israel, ensuring worship and instruction across the nation, and to crown the account of conquest and settlement with the declaration that God gave the land, gave rest, and kept every one of His good promises.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific city allotments describe Israel's historical arrangement. What endures is the theology: God provides for those devoted to His service, worship and His word belong at the center of His people's life everywhere, and, above all, God is perfectly faithful to His promises, a truth for every generation to rest on.

debates.question: How can Joshua 21:43-45 say God gave all the land and rest, when other verses note land unconquered and enemies remaining?

views.label: A true summary of the decisive, God-given gift
views.case: The summary speaks of what God faithfully did: He gave the land as He swore, broke the power of the Canaanite kingdoms, and granted rest from organized war; the remaining pockets do not contradict this, since the essential conquest was accomplished and the land lay open for Israel to occupy, so God's promise stood fulfilled on His side.

views.label: Fulfillment received by faith, with a not-yet remaining
views.case: The land and rest were truly given, yet full possession depended on Israel's continued faith and obedience; the summary affirms God's complete faithfulness while the honest notes of unconquered territory show Israel's incomplete response, and together they point beyond the land to the final rest in Christ that Joshua's rest foreshadowed, Hebrews 4:8-9.

debates.bearing: Both readings uphold the central claim: God kept every promise perfectly; any shortfall lay in Israel's incomplete possession, not in God's word, and the real but partial rest points to the greater rest secured in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's meaning gathers into its final three verses, and three words carry it. First, Israel yarash, took possession of, the land and settled in it; the great conquest verb that has run through the book here comes to rest, for what God gave by promise His people entered and possessed. Second, the LORD gave them nuach, rest on every side, the settled peace toward which the whole story moved, fulfilling His oath to the fathers; yet this rest, real as it was, is not the last word, for it foreshadows the Sabbath rest that still remains for the people of God in Christ, Hebrews 4:8-9. Third, and climactically, not one good dabar, not one word or promise, of all that the LORD had spoken failed; everything was fulfilled. This is the theological summit of the book: God's word is utterly reliable, and the whole account of conquest and settlement stands as proof that what He promises He performs, down to the last detail. Possession, rest, and a word that never fails: the vocabulary declares the faithfulness of God.

typology.type: Levi scattered through the tribes to teach the law and lead worship
typology.fulfillment: God's people as a royal priesthood spread through the world to proclaim His praises, 1 Peter 2:9 and Deuteronomy 33:10.

typology.type: The rest God gave Israel on every side
typology.fulfillment: The Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God, secured in Christ, which the land-rest foreshadowed, Hebrews 4:8-10.

typology.type: Not one of God's good promises failing
typology.fulfillment: Christ, in whom all the promises of God find their Yes and Amen, 2 Corinthians 1:20 and Matthew 5:18.

study.christ: The high point of this chapter, and one of the high points of the whole Old Testament, is the declaration that not one of all the LORD's good promises failed; everything was fulfilled. That faithfulness has a name and a face in the New Testament: all the promises of God find their Yes in Christ, and so through Him we speak our Amen to God for His glory, 2 Corinthians 1:20. Every promise that stands fulfilled here in the gift of the land points forward to the One in whom every promise is finally and fully kept. The rest God gave Israel on every side also points to Him: it was real, but it was not the last rest, for Hebrews tells us that if Joshua had given them final rest, God would not have spoken of another day; so there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God, entered by faith in Christ, Hebrews 4:8-10. And the scattering of the Levites to fill the land with worship and instruction foreshadows the people Christ forms, a royal priesthood spread through the world to declare the praises of Him who called us out of darkness into His marvelous light, 1 Peter 2:9. The faithful God of Joshua 21 is the God who keeps all His promises in His Son.
study.god: This chapter magnifies the faithfulness of God as few passages do. He is the God who provides, settling the landless Levites in cities throughout Israel so that His worship and His word would reach every corner of the nation, and so that those devoted to His service would lack nothing. He is the God who gives, granting Israel the land He swore to their fathers and rest on every side, subduing every enemy who tried to stand against them. And above all He is the God who keeps His word: not one of all His good promises failed; everything was fulfilled. In a book full of human failure and incomplete obedience, the summary fixes attention where it belongs, on the flawless faithfulness of God. What He says, He does; what He swears, He performs. This is the God on whom all faith rests, and whose every promise is secured forever in Christ.

crossReferences.note: The LORD is Levi's portion and inheritance, the reason they received cities rather than a territory.
Jacob foretold Levi being scattered in Israel; here that scattering becomes a blessing, spreading worship and instruction.
Levi teaches Israel God's law and offers His worship, the ministry the scattered cities enabled.
All the promises of God find their Yes in Christ, the fulfillment of not one promise failing.
Joshua's rest was not final; a Sabbath rest remains for the people of God in Christ.

study.oneStory: The landless Levites are settled in forty-eight cities throughout Israel, filling the nation with worship and the teaching of God's law, and the whole account of conquest and settlement is crowned by the declaration that God gave the land, gave rest, and kept every single promise. This unshakeable faithfulness points to Christ, in whom all God's promises are Yes and Amen, and the rest granted to Israel foreshadows the eternal Sabbath rest secured in Him for all His people.

quiz.q: Why did the Levites receive cities scattered among all the tribes rather than a territory of their own?

options.text: Because the LORD was their inheritance, and living among all the tribes let them teach the law and lead worship everywhere.
options.note: Levi's lack of land flowed from their calling to serve God, spread through Israel to minister.

options.text: Because they were being punished and denied a proper inheritance.
options.note: It was not punishment; their inheritance was the LORD Himself, and their scattering served worship and instruction.

options.text: Because they were too few to hold a whole territory.
options.note: The reason was their calling and the LORD as their portion, not their small size.

options.text: Because no tribe wanted them nearby.
options.note: The cities were given by God's command so the Levites could minister among all the tribes, not out of rejection.

quiz.q: How many cities were given to the Levites, and what did they include?

options.text: Forty-eight cities with pasturelands, including the six cities of refuge.
options.note: Verse 41 gives the total of forty-eight, which included the refuge cities.

options.text: Twelve cities, one from each tribe.
options.note: The total was forty-eight cities, not twelve.

options.text: Only the six cities of refuge and nothing else.
options.note: The refuge cities were among the forty-eight, but there were far more than six.

options.text: A single large region east of the Jordan.
options.note: They received scattered cities throughout Israel, not one region.

quiz.q: What does the summary in verses 43 to 45 declare about God?

options.text: That He gave Israel the land and rest, and not one of His good promises failed.
options.note: Verses 43 to 45 are the great declaration of God's perfect faithfulness.

options.text: That God had fulfilled only some of His promises.
options.note: The text says not one promise failed; everything was fulfilled, not merely some.

options.text: That Israel had earned the land by their obedience.
options.note: The land was God's sworn gift, kept by His faithfulness, not earned by Israel.

options.text: That God had abandoned His promises to Israel.
options.note: The opposite: every promise was kept; God proved perfectly faithful.

quiz.q: How does the rest God gave Israel point forward in Scripture?

options.text: To the Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God, secured in Christ.
options.note: Hebrews 4 reads the land-rest as a foreshadowing of the greater rest in Christ.

options.text: To the idea that God's promises finally failed.
options.note: The opposite: the rest shows God's promises fulfilled; it points to a greater rest, not failure.

options.text: To a guarantee that Israel would never struggle again.
options.note: Later struggles came; this rest was real but partial, pointing to the final rest in Christ.
Hebrews draws a direct line from this rest to the Sabbath rest in Christ.

questions: The summary declares that not one of God's good promises failed. Which promise of God do you most need to trust as fully reliable right now?
The Levites were spread through the land so worship and God's word would reach everyone. How are you helping God's truth reach the ordinary places and people around you?
God gave Israel rest on every side, yet a greater rest still lay ahead in Christ. Where are you resting in earthly security when God is inviting you into the deeper rest He gives?
Levi's scattering, once a word of judgment, became a means of blessing. How might God be turning a hard or scattered season of your life into something that blesses others?
In Christ every promise of God is Yes. How does the perfect faithfulness of God, proved in Joshua and fulfilled in Jesus, strengthen your confidence to trust Him today?

translationNotes.note: The forty-eight Levitical cities, including the six cities of refuge, were spread across all the tribes so that priestly ministry reached the whole nation.
The BSB sets off the city totals with em dashes throughout the chapter (for example nine cities, four cities, two cities); these have all been rendered as commas here, with no change to the wording.
Not one of all the LORD's good promises failed is the theological summit of the book, echoed later in Joshua's farewell, 23:14, and fulfilled in Christ, 2 Corinthians 1:20.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 21:1-45. Prose and Levitical city lists; line-break markers within verses were ignored and segments joined with single spaces. The em dashes in the city-total verses (16, 18, 22, 24, 25, 31, 32, 35, 37, 39) were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
